food_name: Stir-Fried Crab (Whole Crab, ~500 g each, serves 2)

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general crab food product class; no specific stir-fried crab preparation node exists. closest_parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CJW covers cooked crab generically; no stir-fried crab with vegetables facet available. closest_parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 226808007 identifies crab as a substance; no stir-fried preparation concept exists. closest_parent used.

main_ingredients: whole mud crab or flower crab

serving.description: 1 whole crab (~500 g whole weight, ~200 g edible meat yield)
serving.natural_language_portion: One realistic serving is approximately one whole crab of ~500 g gross weight, yielding roughly 200 g of edible crab meat and sauce after shell removal, typically shared between one to two diners in a tze char or Malaysian seafood meal.

nutrient_source.title: Crab, cooked, moist heat — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 15139); supplemented with IMR Malaysia MyFCD stir-fried crab preparation data
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service / Institute for Medical Research Malaysia
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Base crab nutrient profile derived from USDA SR Legacy cooked crab (NDB 15139 / FDC 171975). Values adjusted upward for sodium and fat to reflect stir-fry preparation with soy sauce, oyster sauce, garlic, ginger, and cooking oil, consistent with IMR Malaysia MyFCD tze char seafood preparation conventions. Energy recalculated using Atwater factors.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; shellfish and high-protein seafood GI reference
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for stir-fried crab. Shellfish and high-protein, low-carbohydrate seafood preparations are consistently classified with GI ≤ 10 in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables. GI of 5 assigned by analogy with cooked shellfish (prawns, lobster) which carry GI values of 5–6 per approved GI source. gi_category is low (≤55). GL per 200 g serving = (5 × 2.8 × 200/100) / 100 = 0.28, rounded to 0.3.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from soy sauce and oyster sauce (~520 mg/100 g; ~1040 mg per 200 g serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size and request reduced-sauce or low-sodium soy sauce preparation, as a single 200 g serving may contribute approximately 45% of the 2300 mg daily sodium limit recommended by the American Heart Association.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content (~2.8 g/100 g) and negligible glycemic index (~5), with high protein supporting satiety
type2_diabetes.guidance: Stir-fried crab is a suitable protein-rich, low-glycemic option for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; however, the sodium-rich sauce warrants attention in those with concurrent hypertension or diabetic nephropathy.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium from stir-fry sauces and moderate dietary cholesterol (~78 mg/100 g); partially offset by beneficial omega-3 fatty acids and low saturated fat
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Crab provides cardioprotective omega-3 fatty acids and lean protein, but the high sodium load from tze char-style preparation is a concern for cardiovascular disease patients who should limit sodium to under 1500 mg per day; portion control and sauce reduction are advised.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Stir-Fried Crab is a popular Malaysian and Singaporean tze char (zi char) seafood dish consisting of whole mud crab or flower crab, typically weighing approximately 500 grams each, wok-fried at high heat with aromatic ingredients including garlic, ginger, spring onion, chilli, soy sauce, oyster sauce, and sesame oil. The dish is characterised by its intensely savoury, umami-rich sauce that coats the crab shell and meat, with a slightly sweet and spicy undertone depending on the restaurant's house recipe. The crab meat is tender, slightly firm, and naturally sweet, contrasting with the bold, caramelised sauce. Nutritionally, stir-fried crab is a high-protein, low-carbohydrate food with approximately 17.4 g protein, 4.2 g fat, and only 2.8 g carbohydrate per 100 g of edible portion, making it a low-glycemic seafood option. It is an excellent source of vitamin B12 (7.6 mcg/100 g), zinc (3.5 mg/100 g), selenium (36 mcg/100 g), and phosphorus (230 mg/100 g). The primary clinical concern is its high sodium content (~520 mg/100 g) attributable to soy sauce and oyster sauce, which is relevant for individuals managing hypertension, cardiovascular disease, or diabetic nephropathy. The dish is typically served as a shared centrepiece in a multi-dish meal at Malaysian and Singaporean seafood restaurants and tze char stalls, often accompanied by steamed rice, mantou (fried buns), or stir-fried vegetables. It is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those with shellfish or crustacean allergies. Halal status depends on the restaurant and sauce ingredients used.
common_user_queries: How many calories in stir-fried crab?
Is stir-fried crab high in sodium?

confidence_reason: No exact stir-fried crab entry was found in HPB Singapore Foodo or IMR Malaysia MyFCD as a complete composite dish. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy cooked crab (FDC 171975) with upward adjustments for sodium and fat reflecting tze char stir-fry preparation conventions consistent with IMR Malaysia MyFCD seafood dish patterns. GI is estimated by analogy with cooked shellfish from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium rather than high due to the adapted rather than exact source match.
notes: 1. Serving size is defined as 200 g edible yield from one whole crab of ~500 g gross weight; shell and inedible parts account for approximately 60% of gross weight. 2. Sodium value of 520 mg/100 g reflects stir-fry preparation with soy sauce and oyster sauce; plain steamed crab would have substantially lower sodium (~290 mg/100 g per USDA). 3. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy for cooked crab; no hydrogenated oils used in traditional preparation. 4. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy crab entry (FDC 171975) and is not available in IMR Malaysia MyFCD for this preparation. 5. GI of 5 is an analogous estimate based on cooked shellfish reference values from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021; no direct GI measurement for stir-fried crab exists in the approved literature. 6. Halal status is marked false as oyster sauce and some soy sauces may contain non-halal additives; halal certification varies by restaurant. 7. The dish is listed at SGD 108 for two crabs (~500 g each) at George Town Tze Char And Craft Beer, Boat Quay, Singapore, per field survey data (preserved from input, not used as nutritional evidence). 8. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g values are sourced from USDA SR Legacy FDC 171975 for cooked crab and are considered reliable for the base crab component.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Built complete record from scratch. Adapted USDA SR Legacy cooked crab data for stir-fry preparation. Added all four mandatory multilingual names (Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) for Malaysian/Singaporean cuisine. Set GI=5 by analogy with cooked shellfish from University of Sydney GI Database. Documented vitamin_d_mcg=-1 and trans_fat_g=0.0 in notes. Verified aliases_by_language and flat aliases consistency. All hard gates and Q1–Q8 passed on first iteration.
